---
title: "Brain Multiomic A27 CCA Analysis"
author: "Qiuyi Wu"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 2
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(Hmisc)
library(sRDA)
library(PMA)
library(corrplot)
library(dplyr)
library(readxl)
library(ggplot2)
library(ggtext)
library(pheatmap)
library(gridExtra)
library(factoextra)
library(kableExtra)
library(reshape2)
library(deldir)
library(igraph)
library(spdep)
library(patchwork)
library(sRDA)
library(ggrepel)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=TRUE, message=FALSE, cache=FALSE, warning=FALSE)
```


# Data Preprocessing
In this analysis, I proceed with 85% cutoff for batch correction (combat), the PCA analysis, mapping of PCA scores on the brain map. Totally 630 metabolites. After discussion, we decide to remove those missing > 70% for TG metabolites, and remove those missing > 85% for other metabolites. The number of metabolites reduced from 620 to **308**. After meeting in January 2026, we decided to remove the outlier 212. 
Steps for Combat:

1. For TG metabolites, remove those missing > 70%; for other metabolites, remove those missing > 85%, remove outlier 212
2. Impute LOD/2
3. Dilution factor x0.1
5. Log transform
6. Subtract SPQC
7. Combat


For the proteomics data, we followed a procedure similar to that used for the metabolomics data:

- Impute LOD/2
- Dilution factor
- Combat
- Remove heldout samples
- Clustering analysis (on feature variables)


In heldout step, we follow these steps:
1. remove outliers (only Metabolitics data, because Proteomics outliers looks like another batch)
2. comparing the mapped samples and heldout samples and swap them if necessary
3. compute k = (x-med)/mad, for Proteomics and metabolitics data, each of them would have one or two k values (from original samples and heldout samples), and we unselect the one with maximum k values for the final mapping samples

- Use both PC1 and PC2, and compute the robust distance of each sample from the center:
 $$d_i = \sqrt{(PC1_i - med(PC1))^2 + (PC2_i - med(PC2))^2}$$
- Then normalize this by the MAD of the distances:
 $$k_i = \frac{d_i}{MAD(d)}$$
- This way, k represents how far a sample is from the robust center of the PCA space (PC1, PC2).

We took the intersection to retain only the common samples between the two datasets, resulting in 267 samples for the Clustering analysis (PChclust) procedure.

Finally, because the mapping from the sheet `AnalysisID_Matrix` in `A26_Bonnie_Flatmap_Grid.xlsx` contains only 236 samples, **we ultimately obtained 231 matched samples across both datasets**.


# A27 Omics Reconstruction

```{r omics map}
heldout_updated <- as.data.frame(read_excel("../data/Heldout_Updated_a27.xlsx"))
heldout_updated <- heldout_updated[, c(1:3)]
map.df <- data.frame(read_excel("../data/A27_Frizzle_Flatmap_Grid_20260317.xlsx", sheet = "AnalysisID_Matrix", col_names = F))
map.df <- as.matrix(sapply(map.df, as.numeric))
 
load("../data/a27.RData")


# Modify MAP based on HELDOUT: Build a lookup table of all IDs → finalID
id_map <- heldout_updated %>%
  select(Mapped_AnalysisID, HeldOut_AnalysisID1, finalID) %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Mapped_AnalysisID, HeldOut_AnalysisID1),
    values_to = "ID"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(ID)) %>%
  distinct(ID, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
  select(ID, finalID)
# Convert to named vector for fast replacement
lookup <- setNames(id_map$finalID, id_map$ID)

map_char <- as.character(map.df)               # all entries as character for indexing
replacement <- lookup[map_char]                 # yields NA when not found
final_vec <- ifelse(!is.na(replacement), replacement, map_char)  # keep original when not found
map.df<- matrix(as.numeric(final_vec), nrow = nrow(map.df), ncol = ncol(map.df))




load("../data/A27_PChclust.RData")
rownames(a27.pro.cluster$score) <- rownames(a27.met.cluster$score)

get_sample_coordinates <- function(block_matrix) {
  coords <- which(!is.na(block_matrix), arr.ind = TRUE)
  samples <- block_matrix[coords]
  xy_df <- data.frame(
    sample = as.character(samples),
    x = coords[, "col"],  # column index as x
    y = coords[, "row"]   # row index as y
  )
  return(xy_df)
}

map.df <- apply(map.df, 2, as.numeric)
# set 0 value as NA
map.df[map.df  == 0] <- NA

xy_coords <- get_sample_coordinates(apply(map.df, 2, rev))
xy_coords$sample <- as.character(xy_coords$sample)
# Reorder coordinates to match met_design
xy_ordered <- xy_coords[match(rownames(a27.met.cluster$score), xy_coords$sample), c("x", "y")]

# Subset both datasets to common samples
common_samples <- intersect(rownames(a27.met.cluster$score), unique(xy_coords$sample))
met_score_common <- a27.met.cluster$score[common_samples, , drop = FALSE]
pro_score_common <- a27.pro.cluster$score[common_samples, , drop = FALSE]

xy_coords_common <- xy_coords[match(common_samples, xy_coords$sample), c("x", "y")]
rownames(xy_coords_common) <- common_samples

#rownames(xy_ordered) <- xy_coords[match(rownames(a27.met.cluster$score), xy_coords$sample), "sample"]
# remove NA rows (where there's no match between two datasets)
#xy_ordered <- na.omit(xy_ordered)

xy_ordered <- xy_coords_common
```



```{r PCA}
#Function to detect outliers based on median ± k * MAD （detect outliers based on the median ± k × MAD (Median Absolute Deviation)）
mad_outliers <- function(x, k = 10) {
  med <- median(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  m <- mad(x, constant = 1, na.rm = TRUE)   # default constant=1
  out <- x[abs(x - med) > k * m]
  return(out)
}
# Metabolomics
pca_met_corrected <- prcomp(a27.met.data[,-c(1:2)], scale. = TRUE)
pca_met <- data.frame(pca_met_corrected$x)  # PCA scores
pca_met$plate <- a27.met.data$Plate
#pca_met$sample_type <- a27.met.data$sampleType
#pca_met$color_indicator <- ifelse(pca_met$sample_type == "SPQC", "black", pca_met$plate)
pca_met$sample <- as.numeric(a27.met.data$Sample)

# Detect outliers for PC1 and PC2
pc1_outliers <- mad_outliers(pca_met$PC1, k = 7)
pc2_outliers <- mad_outliers(pca_met$PC2, k = 7)
# Subset outliers
outlier_met <- pca_met[pca_met$PC1 %in% pc1_outliers | pca_met$PC2 %in% pc2_outliers, ]


ggplot(pca_met, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=plate)) +
  geom_point(size=2) +
  geom_text_repel(data=outlier_met, aes(label=sample), size=3, color="black") +  # Add outlier labels
  labs(title="PCA Plot of A27 Metabolomics Data After Heldout",
       x="PC 1",
       y="PC 2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("black", "red", "blue", "green", "purple", "orange", "pink")) +  # Set black for SPQC
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(16, 17))  



# Proteomics
pca_pro_corrected <- prcomp(a27.pro.data[,-c(1:3)], scale. = TRUE)
pca_pro <- data.frame(pca_pro_corrected$x)  # PCA scores
pca_pro$plate <- a27.pro.data$pro.plate 
# pca_pro$sample_type <- a26.pro.data$pro.sampletype 
# pca_pro$color_indicator <- ifelse(pca_pro$sample_type == "SPQC", "black", pca_pro$plate)
pca_pro$sample <- as.numeric(a27.pro.data$DukeID)

# Detect outliers for PC1 and PC2
pc1_outliers <- mad_outliers(pca_pro$PC1, k = 7)
pc2_outliers <- mad_outliers(pca_pro$PC2, k = 7)
# Subset outliers
outlier_pro <- pca_pro[pca_pro$PC1 %in% pc1_outliers | pca_pro$PC2 %in% pc2_outliers, ]


ggplot(pca_pro, aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, color=plate)) +
  geom_point(size=2) +
  geom_text_repel(data=outlier_pro, aes(label=sample), size=3, color="black") +  # Add outlier labels
  labs(title="PCA Plot of A27 Proteomics Data After Heldout",
       x="PC 1",
       y="PC 2") +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_color_manual(values=c("black", "red", "blue", "green", "purple", "orange", "pink")) +  # Set black for SPQC
  scale_shape_manual(values=c(16, 17))  


```






![Metabolite Delaunay Network](data/a26network_delaunay_metCluster.png){width=90%}


```{r spCCA cluster 1st component}
a27.met.cluster$score <- a27.met.cluster$score[rownames(a27.met.cluster$score) %in% rownames(xy_ordered), ]
a27.pro.cluster$score <- a27.pro.cluster$score[rownames(a27.pro.cluster$score) %in% rownames(xy_ordered), ]


# spca_delaunay_metCluster <- adegenet::spca(
#   x = a27.met.cluster$score,
#   xy = xy_ordered,
#   center = TRUE,
#   scale = TRUE,
#   scannf = FALSE,
#   nfposi = 3,
#   nfnega = 0
# )
# save(spca_delaunay_metCluster,  file = "../data/sPCA_a27metCluster.RData")
load("../data/sPCA_a27metCluster.RData")

# Standardize both datasets (center + scale)
X <- a27.pro.cluster$score
Y <- a27.met.cluster$score

rownames(X) <- rownames(xy_ordered)

# Get coordinates and rownames
coords <- as.matrix(xy_ordered)
sample_names <- rownames(xy_ordered)



# lw is your listw object
nb <- spca_delaunay_metCluster$lw$neighbours  # adjacency list
# convert to binary adjacency matrix
n <- length(nb)
W_raw <- matrix(0, n, n)
for (i in 1:n) {
  W_raw[i, nb[[i]]] <- 1
}
# degree vector
d_vec <- rowSums(W_raw)
# diagonal degree matrix
D <- diag(d_vec)
# Unnormalized Laplacian
L <- D - W_raw

# Symmetric normalized Laplacian
L_sym <- diag(1 / sqrt(d_vec)) %*% L %*% diag(1 / sqrt(d_vec))


```

## Concept

### 1. CCA
For CCA, let Let $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ and $\mathbf{Y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$ denote the standardized (centered and scaled) feature matrices of two omics datasets—in our case, proteomic cluster scores and metabolomic cluster scores. Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) seeks projection vectors $\mathbf{a}_k \in \mathbb{R}^p$ and $\mathbf{b}_k \in \mathbb{R}^q$ for each component $k \in \{1, \dots, K\}$ such that the canonical variates

$$
\mathbf{u}_k = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{a}_k, \quad \mathbf{v}_k = \mathbf{Y} \mathbf{b}_k
$$

maximize the correlation:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{a}_k, \mathbf{b}_k} \; \text{corr}(\mathbf{X} \mathbf{a}_k, \mathbf{Y} \mathbf{b}_k)
$$

subject to constraints: $\|\mathbf{a}_k\|_2 = 1$, $\|\mathbf{b}_k\|_2 = 1$ and orthogonality across components.


### 2. Sparse CCA
For sparse CCA, it helps to promote interpretability and reduce overfitting in high-dimensional data by imposing sparsity constraints on the canonical vectors. The optimization problem becomes:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{a}_k, \mathbf{b}_k} \; \mathbf{a}_k^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{Y} \mathbf{b}_k \quad \text{subject to} \quad
\|\mathbf{a}_k\|_2 \leq 1,\;
\|\mathbf{b}_k\|_2 \leq 1,\;
\|\mathbf{a}_k\|_1 \leq c_1,\;
\|\mathbf{b}_k\|_1 \leq c_2
$$

Here, $c_1$ and $c_2$ are sparsity parameters controlling how many features contribute to each canonical direction. We estimate the sparse canonical vectors using the `PMA::CCA()` function.


### 3. Spatial Sparse CCA

To account for spatial structure in the samples, we pre-transform the data using a spatial smoothing matrix. Let $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ be a spatial weight matrix derived from Delaunay triangulation (via sPCA). We compute its eigendecomposition:

$$
\mathbf{W} = \mathbf{V} \mathbf{\Lambda} \mathbf{V}^\top
$$

Then define the symmetric square root:

$$
\mathbf{W}^{1/2} = \mathbf{V} \; \text{diag}(\sqrt{\lambda_1^+}, \dots, \sqrt{\lambda_n^+}) \; \mathbf{V}^\top
$$

where $\lambda_i^+ = \max(\lambda_i, 0)$.

We apply this transformation to both datasets:

$$
\tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{W}^{1/2} \mathbf{X}, \quad
\tilde{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{W}^{1/2} \mathbf{Y}
$$

We then scale these and run sparse CCA on the spatially informed versions $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{Y}}$.


## Visualization 

For each component $k = 1, \dots, K$:

### 1. Canonical Scores (Spatial Maps)

The spatially transformed canonical scores

$$
\mathbf{u}_k = \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{a}_k, \quad \mathbf{v}_k = \tilde{\mathbf{Y}} \mathbf{b}_k
$$

are mapped onto 2D sample locations using heatmaps (via `ggplot2::geom_tile()`). These maps highlight spatial patterns in the scores associated with each component.

### 2. Feature-Level Contributions

We compute feature-level contributions by projecting the canonical vectors back through the cluster loading matrices:

$$
\text{Proteomic feature weights} = \mathbf{L}_X \mathbf{a}_k, \quad
\text{Metabolomic feature weights} = \mathbf{L}_Y \mathbf{b}_k
$$

The top 20 features (by absolute value) are displayed using barplots for interpretation.

### 3. Cluster-Level Contributions

The vectors $\mathbf{a}_k$ and $\mathbf{b}_k$ themselves indicate the importance of proteomic and metabolomic clusters. Their top 20 values (absolute) are also plotted.




## Spatial CCA {.tabset}
We use `{PMA}::CCA()` for sparse CCA. It's purely designed for statistical association and sparsity between two datasets, without knowledge of space (e.g., neighbor structure via spatial inverse laplacian weight matrix $\widetilde{W} = L^{-1}$). 

$$L = I - D^{-1/2}wD^{1/2}$$



$$\widetilde{W} = (L + \epsilon I)^{-1} =\frac{1}{\epsilon} (\frac{1}{\epsilon} L +  I)^{-1}\approx (\eta L+ I)^{-1} = Q(\eta\Lambda + I)^{-1} Q^{\top}$$
where $\eta = \frac{1}{\epsilon} =  \frac{1}{median(\lambda)}$


Here, we preprocess the data as $\widetilde{W}^{1/2}X$ and $\widetilde{W}^{1/2}Y$ to indirectly incorporate spatial information. This approach is valid because $\widetilde{W}$ is symmetric and positive semi-definite, so that $\widetilde{W}^{1/2}$ exists.



```{r sparse spCCA 2, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='hide', fig.width=4, fig.height=4}

# Inverse Laplacian (Moore–Penrose with small reg)
eig <- eigen(L_sym, symmetric = TRUE)
epsilon <- median(eig$values)  #max(eig$values) 
eta <- 1/epsilon
eig_inv_vals <- 1 / (eta*eig$values + 1)            # ifelse(eig$values > eps, 1 / eig$values, 0)
W_tilde <- eig$vectors %*% diag(eig_inv_vals) %*% t(eig$vectors)   # = L_sym^{-1}



## PLOT ##
val_median <- median(eig$values)
val_max <- max(eig$values)
idx_median <- which.min(abs(eig$values - val_median))
idx_max <- which.max(eig$values)

plot(eig$values, pch = 20, main = "Eigenvalue of L")
points(idx_median, val_median, col = "red", pch = 19, cex = 1.5)
points(idx_max, val_max, col = "red", pch = 19, cex = 1.5)
text(idx_median, val_median, 
     labels = round(val_median, 3), 
     pos = 4, col = "red")
text(idx_max, val_max, 
     labels = round(val_max, 3), 
     pos = 4, col = "red")
##########

eig <- eigen(W_tilde)
W_half <- eig$vectors %*% diag(sqrt(pmax(eig$values, 0.01))) %*% t(eig$vectors)
W_half_inv  <- eig$vectors %*% diag(1/sqrt(pmax(eig$values, 0.01)), nrow=length(eig$values)) %*% t(eig$vectors)


X_tilde <- W_half %*% X
Y_tilde <- W_half %*% Y

Xtc <- X_tilde
Ytc <- Y_tilde

set.seed(123)
perm_out <- CCA.permute(Xtc, Ytc, typex = "standard", typez = "standard", nperms = 25, standardize = F)

# Extract optimal penalties
penaltyx <- perm_out$bestpenaltyx
penaltyz <- perm_out$bestpenaltyz


scca_res <- CCA(Xtc, Ytc, typex = "standard", typez = "standard",
               K = 10, penaltyx = penaltyx, penaltyz = penaltyz)

```

<!-- ![Average Correlation](data/averageCorrelation.png){width=90%} -->


- U: canonical variate values for X (proteomics) for each subject. Dimension: n × K (n subjects, K components).
- V: canonical variate values for Y (metabolomics) for each subject. Dimension: n × K (n subjects, K components).
- P: loadings vector for X (how much each X feature contributes to a canonical component). Dimension: p × K (p features from X).
- Q: loadings vector for Y (how much each Y feature contributes). Dimension: p × K (p features from X)



```{r wrap function}
plot_spcca_component <- function(i, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster) {
  ai <- scca_res$u[, i]
  bi <- scca_res$v[, i]
  
  u_scores <- Xtc %*% ai
  v_scores <- Ytc %*% bi
  names(u_scores) <- rownames(X)
  names(v_scores) <- rownames(Y)
  
  u_unsmoothed <- W_half_inv %*% u_scores
  v_unsmoothed <- W_half_inv %*% v_scores
  names(u_unsmoothed) <- rownames(X)
  names(v_unsmoothed) <- rownames(Y)
  
  u_scores.cluster = u_df.cluster = p_u.cluster = vector(length = 3, "list")

  

  cat("Component: ", i, "\n")
  cat("Pearson correlation: ", round(cor(u_scores, v_scores), 4), "\n")
  cat("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor(u_scores, v_scores, method = "spearman"), 4), "\n")

  # Spatial maps
  u_matrix <- matrix(u_scores[match(map.df, names(u_scores))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  u_df <- reshape2::melt(apply(u_matrix, 2, rev), varnames = c("Row", "Column"), 
                         value.name = paste0("spCCA_U", i))
  v_matrix <- matrix(v_scores[match(map.df, names(v_scores))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  v_df <- reshape2::melt(apply(v_matrix, 2, rev), varnames = c("Row", "Column"), 
                         value.name = paste0("spCCA_V", i))
  

  # p_u <- ggplot(u_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_U", i))) +
  #   geom_tile(color = "black") + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  #   coord_fixed() + theme_minimal() +
  #   labs(title = paste0("spCCA Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), fill = "Score")
  # 
  # p_v <- ggplot(v_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_V", i))) +
  #   geom_tile(color = "black") + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  #   coord_fixed() + theme_minimal() +
  #   labs(title = paste0("spCCA Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), fill = "Score")
  

  p_u <- ggplot(u_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_U", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("spCCA Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())
  
  p_v <- ggplot(v_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_V", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("spCCA Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())
  
  
  print(p_u.cluster[[1]] + p_u.cluster[[2]] + p_u.cluster[[3]] ) 
  print(p_v + p_u)
  
  
  cat("Component: ", i, "\n")
  cat("Pearson correlation: ", round(cor(u_unsmoothed, v_unsmoothed), 4), "\n")
  cat("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor(u_unsmoothed, v_unsmoothed, method = "spearman"), 4), "\n")

  # unsmoothed Spatial maps
  u_matrix2 <- matrix(u_unsmoothed[match(map.df, names(u_unsmoothed))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  u_df2 <- reshape2::melt(apply(u_matrix2, 2, rev), varnames = c("Row", "Column"), 
                       value.name = paste0("spCCA_U", i))
  v_matrix2 <- matrix(v_unsmoothed[match(map.df, names(v_unsmoothed))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  v_df2 <- reshape2::melt(apply(v_matrix2, 2, rev), varnames = c("Row", "Column"), 
                       value.name = paste0("spCCA_V", i))

  # p_u2 <- ggplot(u_df2, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_U", i))) +
  # geom_tile(color = "black") + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  # coord_fixed() + theme_minimal() +
  # labs(title = paste0("Unsmoothed Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), fill = "Score")
  # 
  # p_v2 <- ggplot(v_df2, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_V", i))) +
  # geom_tile(color = "black") + scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  # coord_fixed() + theme_minimal() +
  # labs(title = paste0("Unsmoothed Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), fill = "Score")
  
  p_u2 <- ggplot(u_df2, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_U", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("Unsmoothed spCCA Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())
  
  p_v2 <- ggplot(v_df2, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("spCCA_V", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("Unsmoothed spCCA Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())
  

  print(p_v2 + p_u2)


  # Feature-level weights
  pro_feat_wt <- a27.pro.cluster$loading %*% ai
  met_feat_wt <- a27.met.cluster$loading %*% bi
  names(pro_feat_wt) <- rownames(a27.pro.cluster$loading)
  names(met_feat_wt) <- rownames(a27.met.cluster$loading)

  barplot(sort(abs(pro_feat_wt), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20], las = 2, cex.names = 0.5,
          main = paste0("Top Proteomic Features U", i))
  barplot(sort(abs(met_feat_wt), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20], las = 2, cex.names = 0.5,
          main = paste0("Top Metabolomic Features V", i))

  names(ai) <- colnames(X)
  names(bi) <- colnames(Y)
  barplot(sort(abs(ai), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20], las = 2, cex.names = 0.6,
          main = paste0("Top Proteomic Clusters U", i))
  barplot(sort(abs(bi), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20], las = 2, cex.names = 0.6,
          main = paste0("Top Metabolomic Clusters V", i))
  

  
  
}


   
# pro_tb <- as.data.frame(scca_res$u)
# pro_tb <- cbind(Cluster = colnames(X), pro_tb)
# pro.select.cluster <- pro_tb[pro_tb$Cluster %in% cluster_id,]


# #write_xlsx(pro_tb,"pro_cluster.xlsx")
# 
# met_tb <- as.data.frame(scca_res$v)
# met_tb <- cbind(Cluster = colnames(Y), met_tb)
# 
# #write_xlsx(met_tb,"met_cluster.xlsx")
# 
# group.met <- a27.met.cluster$group
# loading.met <- a27.met.cluster$loading
# df.met <- data.frame(
#     Feature = rownames(loading.met),
#     Cluster = group.met[rownames(loading.met)],
#     Loading = apply(loading.met, 1, function(x) x[x!=0]))
# 
# df.met <- df.met[order(df.met$Cluster, -abs(df.met$Loading)), ]
# df.met <- df.met %>%
#     dplyr::group_by(Cluster) %>%
#     dplyr::mutate(Contribution = abs(Loading) / sum(abs(Loading))) %>%
#     dplyr::ungroup()
# write_xlsx(df.met,"met_cluster_contribution.xlsx")
# 
# 
# 
# group.pro <- a27.pro.cluster$group
# loading.pro <- a27.pro.cluster$loading
# df.pro <- data.frame(
#   Feature = rownames(loading.pro),
#   Cluster = group.pro[rownames(loading.pro)],
#   Loading = apply(loading.pro, 1, function(x) x[x!=0]))
# 
# df.pro <- df.pro[order(df.pro$Cluster, -abs(df.pro$Loading)), ]
# df.pro <- df.pro %>%
#   dplyr::group_by(Cluster) %>%
#   dplyr::mutate(Contribution = abs(Loading) / sum(abs(Loading))) %>%
#   dplyr::ungroup()
# write_xlsx(df.pro,"pro_cluster_contribution.xlsx")


```


### Component 1

```{r c1 }
plot_spcca_component(1, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 2

```{r c2}
plot_spcca_component(2, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 3

```{r c3}
plot_spcca_component(3, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```



### Component 4

```{r c4}
plot_spcca_component(4, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```




### Component 5

```{r c5}
plot_spcca_component(5, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```



### Component 6

```{r c6}
plot_spcca_component(6, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 7

```{r c7}
plot_spcca_component(7, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 8

```{r c8}
plot_spcca_component(8, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 9

```{r c9}
plot_spcca_component(9, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 10

```{r c10}
plot_spcca_component(10, scca_res, Xtc, Ytc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```







## CCA (without Spatial Information) {.tabset}
In `PMA::CCA`, the canonical correlations are not guaranteed to be in decreasing order by default.

- No orthogonality constraint: Sparse CCA (`PMA::CCA`) does not enforce orthogonality between canonical components like regular CCA does. As a result, later components can have higher correlation than earlier ones.

- Greedy optimization: Each pair of sparse canonical vectors is obtained through an iterative penalized optimization, which may not find the global optimum or follow a strict ordering.


```{r CCA (without Spatial Information), message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, echo=FALSE, results='hide'}

X <- a27.pro.cluster$score
Y <- a27.met.cluster$score

# standardize data
Xc <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)
Yc <- scale(Y, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE)


set.seed(123)
perm_out <- CCA.permute(Xc, Yc, typex = "standard", typez = "standard", nperms = 25)

# Extract optimal penalties
penaltyx <- perm_out$bestpenaltyx
penaltyz <- perm_out$bestpenaltyz


cca_res <- CCA(Xc, Yc, typex = "standard", typez = "standard",
               K = 10, penaltyx = penaltyx, penaltyz = penaltyz)


# Compute correlations manually
cors <- sapply(1:ncol(cca_res$u), function(i) cor(Xc %*% cca_res$u[, i], Yc %*% cca_res$v[, i]))

# Order components by decreasing correlation
order_idx <- order(abs(cors), decreasing = TRUE)

```

```{r wrap function2}
plot_cca_component <- function(i, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster) {
    # Canonical vectors (loadings)
  ai <- cca_res$u[, i]
  bi <- cca_res$v[, i]

  # Canonical scores
  u_scores <- Xc %*% ai
  v_scores <- Yc %*% bi
  names(u_scores) <- rownames(X)
  names(v_scores) <- rownames(Y)

  # Correlations
  cat("Component: ", i, "\n")
  cat("Pearson correlation: ", round(cor(u_scores, v_scores, method = "pearson"), 4), "\n")
  cat("Spearman correlation: ", round(cor(u_scores, v_scores, method = "spearman"), 4), "\n")

  # Prepare spatial maps
  u_matrix <- matrix(u_scores[match(map.df, names(u_scores))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  u_matrix_flip <- apply(u_matrix, 2, rev)
  u_df <- melt(u_matrix_flip, varnames = c("Row", "Column"), value.name = paste0("CCA_U", i))

  v_matrix <- matrix(v_scores[match(map.df, names(v_scores))], nrow = nrow(map.df))
  v_matrix_flip <- apply(v_matrix, 2, rev)
  v_df <- melt(v_matrix_flip, varnames = c("Row", "Column"), value.name = paste0("CCA_V", i))

  # p_u <- ggplot(u_df, aes_string(x = "Column", y = "Row", fill = paste0("CCA_U", i))) +
  # geom_tile(color = "black") +
  # scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") + #, limits = c(-15, 15), oob = scales::squish) +
  # coord_fixed() +
  # theme_minimal() +
  # labs(title = paste0("Sparse spCCA Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), fill = "Score") +
  # theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12))
  # 
  # p_v <- ggplot(v_df, aes_string(x = "Column", y = "Row", fill = paste0("CCA_V", i))) +
  # geom_tile(color = "black") +
  # scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma") +
  # coord_fixed() +
  # theme_minimal() +
  # labs(title = paste0("Sparse spCCA Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), fill = "Score") +
  # theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12))
  
  p_u <- ggplot(u_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("CCA_U", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("Sparse spCCA Proteomic Score (U", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())

  p_v <- ggplot(v_df, aes_string("Column", "Row", fill = paste0("CCA_V", i))) +
  geom_raster() + coord_equal() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(title = paste0("Sparse spCCA Metabolomic Score (V", i, ")"), x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Score") +
  theme_minimal() + theme( panel.grid = element_blank())

  print(p_v + p_u)

  # Feature-level contributions
  proteomic_feature_weights <- a27.pro.cluster$loading %*% ai
  metabolomic_feature_weights <- a27.met.cluster$loading %*% bi
  names(proteomic_feature_weights) <- rownames(a27.pro.cluster$loading)
  names(metabolomic_feature_weights) <- rownames(a27.met.cluster$loading)

  top_pro_features <- sort(abs(proteomic_feature_weights), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20]
  barplot(top_pro_features, las = 2, cex.names = 0.5, main = paste0("Top Proteomic Features in Sparse CCA U", i))

  top_met_features <- sort(abs(metabolomic_feature_weights), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20]
  barplot(top_met_features, las = 2, cex.names = 0.5, main = paste0("Top Metabolomic Features in Sparse CCA V", i))


  # Cluster-level contributions
  names(ai) <- colnames(X)
  names(bi) <- colnames(Y)
  top_pro_clusters <- sort(abs(ai), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20]
  top_met_clusters <- sort(abs(bi), decreasing = TRUE)[1:20]

  barplot(top_pro_clusters, las = 2, cex.names = 0.6,
  main = paste0("Top Proteomic Clusters in Sparse CCA U", i))
  barplot(top_met_clusters, las = 2, cex.names = 0.6,
  main = paste0("Top Metabolomic Clusters in Sparse CCA V", i))
}
```


### Component 1

```{r cc1}
plot_cca_component(1, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 2
```{r cc2}
plot_cca_component(2, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 3
```{r cc3}
plot_cca_component(3, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 4
```{r cc4}
plot_cca_component(4, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 5
```{r cc5}
plot_cca_component(5, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 6
```{r cc6}
plot_cca_component(6, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 7
```{r cc7}
plot_cca_component(7, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 8
```{r cc8}
plot_cca_component(8, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```

### Component 9
```{r cc9}
plot_cca_component(9, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```


### Component 10
```{r cc10}
plot_cca_component(10, cca_res, Xc, Yc, map.df, X, Y, a27.pro.cluster, a27.met.cluster)
```
